title: 27 Tahsas — The Massacre of the Innocents & The Assembly
narrative:
The twenty-seventh day of Tahsas commemorates the Holy Innocents, those infant boys of Bethlehem and its environs who were slaughtered by the command of King Herod the Great in his murderous attempt to destroy the Christ Child. The Church has always honored these children as martyrs — indeed, as the first martyrs of the new dispensation, the flowers of the Church before the Church had fully blossomed, who died for Christ without the capacity to understand what they died for, giving their lives in the place of the One who would later give His life for all. Their number, traditionally reckoned at fourteen thousand in the Eastern tradition though the historical reality was certainly more modest, expresses the theological enormity of what was done: the Incarnate Lord arrived among His own people and the world's response was the murder of children.

The monthly commemoration of the Crucifixion of Our Lord falls on this day, and its juxtaposition with the Massacre of the Innocents is theologically devastating in its precision. The infants of Bethlehem die that the Christ Child might live; the Christ Child lives that He might grow to die on the cross for all the children of Adam. The blood of the Innocents at the beginning of the Lord's earthly life and the blood of the Lord on the cross at the end of it form two banks of the same river: the river of redemptive suffering that flows through the entire economy of salvation. Herod sheds the blood of the innocent thinking to prevent salvation; God permits the shedding of innocent blood knowing that it will become itself a participation in the great act of salvation that the Innocent One will accomplish on Calvary.

Saint Stephen the Protomartyr, whose monthly commemoration appears again on this day, stands as the adult counterpart of the Holy Innocents: as they died without knowledge of the one for whom they died, he died in full knowledge and full freedom, seeing the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God. The Coptic and Ethiopian churches have always placed Stephen close to the Nativity season, for his martyrdom is the first human echo of the divine self-offering that the Child of Bethlehem came to accomplish. The crown that his name promises was the first crown given after the Nativity; the innocents' crowns were given at the same time, by the same sword of Herodian wickedness.

The theological seal of this twenty-seventh day of Tahsas is the mystery of innocent suffering as participation in the redemptive mystery. The Innocents suffer innocently, as the Innocent One will suffer on the cross; Stephen suffers willingly, as the willing victim of Calvary; the monthly Crucifixion commemoration draws both into the eternal act of the self-giving God. The Incarnation that Tahsas celebrates is not a comfortable mystery of warmth and light in isolation: it is a mystery of light entering darkness, of divine love entering the world's violence, of the innocent Lamb coming among wolves — and being, in the end, more powerful than all the wolves in their fury.
